text.title: RAG Pipeline Design: Production Deployment
text.summary: Production checklist, caching layers, monitoring metrics, security, cost management, and evaluation with ragas.

rule.statement: Implement three caching layers: embedding cache (Redis, TTL based on query patterns), semantic cache (similar query matching at 0.95+ cosine similarity), and response cache for identical queries.
rule.rationale: Embedding calls cost money and add latency. Query patterns in production are highly repetitive — 40-60% cache hit rates are common. Semantic caching captures near-duplicate queries that differ only in phrasing.

rule.statement: Hash document content and skip re-embedding unchanged documents during incremental index updates. Never re-embed the full corpus unless the embedding model changes.
rule.rationale: Embedding is expensive. A 10K-document corpus costs $5-50 to embed depending on model. Re-embedding unchanged documents wastes budget and time. Content hash comparison adds negligible overhead.

rule.statement: Track query latency (p50/p95/p99), retrieval score distribution, cache hit rate, and token usage per query in production. Monitor retrieval score drift — score degradation below 0.6 average indicates embedding model mismatch or data drift.
rule.rationale: RAG quality degrades silently. Score distribution monitoring catches data drift before users notice quality degradation. Latency breakdowns per component (embed → search → rerank → LLM) reveal where to optimize.

list.item: Retrieval recall@10: target >85% (measure with ragas weekly)
Cache hit rate: track per caching layer

rule.statement: Run ragas evaluation against 50-100 labeled QA pairs before every production release. Gate deployment on faithfulness above 0.9 and context recall above 0.85.
rule.rationale: ragas provides automated faithfulness (grounding in context) and relevancy (answer matches question) metrics using LLM-as-judge. Running it before every release prevents quality regressions from undetected chunking or prompt changes.

example.code:
from ragas import evaluate from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_recall from datasets import Dataset
eval_dataset = Dataset.from_list([ {"question": q, "answer": a, "contexts": ctx, "ground_truth": gt} for q, a, ctx, gt in labeled_qa_pairs ]) result = evaluate(eval_dataset, metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_recall]) assert result["faithfulness"] >= 0.90, f"Faithfulness below threshold: {result['faithfulness']}" assert result["context_recall"] >= 0.85, f"Context recall below threshold: {result['context_recall']}"

rule.statement: Multi-tenant RAG must isolate namespaces strictly via collection partitioning or namespace filters. A missing filter parameter in a query causes data leakage across tenants.
rule.rationale: Vector database queries without tenant filters return results from all tenants. This is a data leakage risk that is invisible in logs — the query succeeds and returns results, just results from the wrong tenant. Always make tenant_id a required parameter in the search function.

antipattern.description: Injecting retrieved context verbatim without length limits into the LLM prompt.
antipattern.reason: Long chunks eat context budget fast. 5 chunks of 1000 tokens each plus system prompt may consume 80% of a 8K context model. Compress or truncate retrieved passages before injection; prioritize by reranking score.
